This means children get insufficient practice at both the easy confidence-building level (D1) and the challenging exam-discriminating level (D3). The D2 block from Q31-Q128 is particularly repetitive with minor variations on the same word combinations.
highIssues.recommendation: Target approximately D1:38, D2:52, D3:38 for 128 questions. Add more D1 questions with short common words and obvious first-letter removals. Add more D3 questions with 6-7 letter words, non-obvious letter positions, less common vocabulary, and right-to-left direction.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q22 PLIGHT+OUR rated D3 but is a simple first-letter removal
highIssues.description: PLIGHT -> LIGHT is a straightforward first-letter removal. OUR -> POUR is a common word. Both resulting words are high-frequency. This should be D1 or at most D2. The only slightly harder element is PLIGHT being 6 letters, but the P removal is the most obvious move. Per Oracle specs, D3 requires '6-7 letter words, non-obvious letter position, less common vocabulary'.
highIssues.recommendation: Re-rate to D1 (or D2 at most). Reserve D3 for questions where the letter position is genuinely non-obvious or the resulting words are uncommon.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q15 mapped to 'Moving the First Letter' but answer is middle letter R
highIssues.description: Q15 (SHORE+INK, answer R) is mapped to sub-concept 'remove-first-letter' with high confidence. However, R is not the first letter of SHORE — S is. The R is the 4th letter (middle). This should be mapped to 'remove-middle-letter'. The mapping algorithm appears to have blindly assigned all questions with a remove-from-word-1 pattern to 'first letter' regardless of actual position.
highIssues.recommendation: Re-map to 'remove-middle-letter' sub-concept. Audit all other mappings to check position accuracy.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Over-reliance on tiny receiving words (OAR, OAK, AGE, ATE, EAR, OWL)
highIssues.description: A huge proportion of questions use 2-3 letter receiving words like OAR (appears 15+ times), OAK (5+ times), AGE (8+ times), EAR (6+ times), ATE (4+ times), OWL (3+ times). Real GL papers use more varied and longer receiving words. This creates a pattern where children learn 'if the small word starts with a vowel, add a consonant to the front' rather than genuinely developing the remove-and-check skill.
highIssues.recommendation: Diversify receiving words. Use 4-5 letter words as receivers too. Include examples where the moved letter goes to the middle or end of the receiving word, not just the start.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Repetitive base word reuse — SCOLD appears in 9 questions, FLAME in 8, STALE in 8
highIssues.description: Rather than creating unique word pairs, the bank systematically pairs 4-5 base words (SCOLD, PRICE, FLAME, STALE, STERN, STORE) with many different small receiving words. This produces Q31-51 (SCOLD variants), Q34-58 (PRICE variants), Q59-72 (FLAME/STALE variants). A child seeing SCOLD for the 9th time learns nothing new about letter-move strategy.
highIssues.recommendation: Each question should use a unique source word. There are thousands of valid English words for letter-move puzzles — no need to reuse.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q1 and Q9 are identical questions — both ask A, C, E, G, ___
highIssues.description: Q1 (D2) asks 'What comes next in this letter series? A, C, E, G, ___' with answer I. Q9 (D1) asks the exact same question with the exact same answer. The options differ slightly (Q1: H,I,J,K,L; Q9: H,I,J,K,L — actually identical options too). Having the same question twice wastes a slot and could appear in the same quiz session. Q1 is rated D2 and Q9 is rated D1, which itself is inconsistent — the same question can't be two different difficulties.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Remove Q9 entirely (keep Q1 which has the better difficulty rating of D2 — a simple skip-one pattern is arguably low-D2). Alternatively, change Q9 to a different D1 pattern like 'A, B, C, D, ___' or 'B, D, F, H, ___' (though the latter already exists as Q3 and Q10).
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q3 and Q10 are identical questions — both ask B, D, F, H, ___
highIssues.description: Q3 (D1) asks 'What comes next in this letter series? B, D, F, H, ___' with answer J. Q10 (D1) asks the exact same question with the same answer. Both are rated D1. Options are slightly different (Q3: I,J,K,L,M; Q10: I,K,L,J,M) but the question and answer are identical. This is a direct duplicate.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Remove Q10. Replace with a different D1 single-letter series such as 'C, E, G, I, ___' (K) or 'D, F, H, J, ___' (L).
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: D1 under-represented at 22% (target 30%)
highIssues.description: Only 28 of 128 questions (22%) are D1. The GL target is ~30% (38 questions). This means children encounter harder questions sooner than intended, which can be discouraging for a topic that already requires strong alphabet-position knowledge. The 10 missing D1 questions would provide more practice at the foundational 'calculate word value' skill before progressing to comparisons and mixed operations.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Add 10 D1 questions: 5 simple 3-letter word values (using early-alphabet letters A-M for smaller numbers), 3 'what number does letter X represent' questions, and 2 simple A+B+C addition problems. This brings D1 to 38/138 (28%), close to target.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q5 rated D2 but is a simple D1 word value question
highIssues.description: Q5: 'If A=1, B=2, C=3... what is the total value of the word ICE?' Answer: I=9, C=3, E=5 = 17. This is a straightforward 3-letter word value addition using early-alphabet letters — identical in structure to Q1 (CAT=24), Q2 (BED=11), Q3 (AGE=13), all of which are D1. There is no reason for ICE to be D2. The letters I, C, E are all in the first half of the alphabet.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Downgrade to D1. This would also help address the D1 under-representation issue.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Rhyming synonym puzzles incorrectly mapped to 'Words with Multiple Meanings'
highIssues.description: Q5 ('overweight feline' = Fat cat), Q11 ('large swine' = Big pig), Q17 ('angry employer' = Cross boss) are rhyming synonym puzzles — a distinct GL question type where the child must find two rhyming words that match a definition. These are mapped to the 'homonyms' sub-concept ('Words with Multiple Meanings'), which teaches about words like 'bank' having two meanings. The lesson content (about recognising multiple definitions from context) has nothing to do with solving rhyming synonym puzzles. A child who studies the homonyms lesson will not learn how to solve these questions.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Create a new sub-concept 'Rhyming Synonym Puzzles' with a lesson teaching the strategy: (1) identify the definition words, (2) find a synonym for each, (3) check the synonyms rhyme. Map Q5, Q11, Q17, and any other rhyming pair questions to this new sub-concept.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q6 (3-person ordering) mapped to 'Ordering 4 People'
highIssues.description: Q6: 'Tom is older than Sam. Sam is older than Jack. Who is the youngest?' involves only 3 people but is mapped to the 'four-person-ordering' sub-concept. The lesson for 4-person ordering teaches about finding the 'link name' that connects two clues — unnecessary for 3-person problems where the connection is always obvious. This should be mapped to a 3-person ordering sub-concept or the 'superlative-questions' sub-concept.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Remap Q6 to 'superlative-questions' (Who Is Tallest / Shortest / Oldest?) which is the correct sub-concept for 3-person extreme-position questions.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: 10 questions have no lesson mapping
highIssues.description: The mapping meta shows 10 unmapped questions out of 163. These questions are likely calendar/sequence questions (Q16 'What month comes two months after March?', Q20 'What day comes three days after Monday?'), arithmetic-logic hybrids (Q12 'Rose has twice as many as Lily'), or unusual question formats that don't fit existing sub-concepts. Unmapped questions mean the child gets no micro-lesson preparation before encountering them.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Identify the 10 unmapped questions and either: (a) create new sub-concepts for genuine gaps (calendar reasoning, arithmetic-logic), or (b) map them to the closest existing sub-concept with a note about the imperfect fit.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Mechanical filler distractors (TIP, MOB, HUB, JAR) appear across 60+ questions
highIssues.description: Questions Q16-Q100 systematically use the same filler options: TIP, MOB, HUB, JAR appear in almost every question as wrong answers. These are transparently not related to the target word and offer no learning value. A child quickly learns to ignore any option containing TIP, MOB, HUB, or JAR, making the questions trivially easy regardless of the actual word. GL-style distractors should be plausible alternatives that test whether the child can distinguish similar letter combinations.
highIssues.recommendation: Replace filler distractors with plausible alternatives. For Q16 (FORE___=FOREMAN), good distractors would be MAT (FOREMAT?), MAP (FOREMAP?), MEN (FOREMEN — real but different word), MAX (FOREMAX?). Each distractor should at least look like it could complete a word.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q101-Q125 block has answer always in position 0 (first option)
highIssues.description: Every question from Q101 to Q125 has the correct answer as the first option (correct: 0). This is a massive pattern that makes these questions trivially exploitable. A child who notices this pattern can get 25 questions right by always picking A. GL papers distribute answer positions evenly.
highIssues.recommendation: Randomise the answer position across all 5 positions for Q101-Q125. Each position should appear roughly 5 times across 25 questions.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: No sentence-context questions despite Oracle listing this as a sub-type
highIssues.description: The Oracle specification lists sub-type 4 as: 'In sentence context: The children GATED → HER (gatHERed)'. This format appears on GL papers but is completely absent from the question bank. Sentence-context questions are harder because children must first identify the complete word from context, then find the three-letter word within it.
highIssues.recommendation: Add 10-15 sentence-context questions across D2 and D3. Example: 'The ship sailed across the ___(?)___ER.' Answer: HAR (HARBOUR).
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q60 answer is -12, introducing negative numbers unexpectedly
highIssues.description: Q60: '543, 432, 321, 210, 99, ___' with answer -12 (pattern is -111). This is rated D2 but the answer involves a negative number. While the VR Number Series Oracle spec does not explicitly exclude negatives, the GL Assessment typically does not require negative number answers in the VR paper. Negative numbers are a separate maths topic. A child who correctly identifies the -111 pattern might still get confused by the negative answer, which is a maths skill issue rather than a pattern recognition issue. Additionally, all five options are negative, which removes the 'is the answer negative?' decision from the problem — a real GL question would mix positive and negative options to test understanding.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Either: (a) Replace with a series where -111 yields a small positive answer (e.g., 654, 543, 432, 321, 210, ___  → answer 99, which is already Q60's penultimate term — so use different starting numbers), or (b) move to D3 and include a mix of positive and negative options to genuinely test whether the child understands the answer should be negative.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q8 correct index points to wrong option
highIssues.description: Q8: 'If BIRD = 1234, what is the code for RIB?' Options: ['312', '321', '231', '123', '213']. correct=1 (index 1 = '321') and correctAnswer='321'. However, from BIRD=1234: B=1, I=2, R=3, D=4. RIB = R(3) I(2) B(1) = 321. The correct answer IS 321 which is at index 1. Wait — on re-check, correct=1 means options[1]='321', and correctAnswer='321'. But the explanation says 'From BIRD=1234: B=1, I=2, R=3, D=4. RIB = R(3) I(2) B(1) = 321' which is correct. However, the 'correct' field is 1, but options[0]='312' and options[1]='321'. Actually this IS correct: index 1 = '321'. But the explanation mentions 'the first digit must be 3' and says to 'cross out options that don't start with 3', while options[0]='312' also starts with 3 — the elimination tip is misleading in this context since two options start with 3.
highIssues.suggestedFix: The answer itself is verified correct (321, index 1). However, the tip 'Use elimination — if you know the first digit must be 3, cross out options that don't start with 3' is misleading because option '312' also starts with 3. Replace this tip with one that's relevant to the question at hand.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Tip about double letters appears on questions with no double letters
highIssues.description: The tip 'Start by finding double letters — if a word has OO and a code has the same digit twice, that's your entry point!' appears on at least 10 questions where neither the source word nor the target word contains any repeated letters. For example, Q1 (SPOT/TOPS), Q9 (STAR/RATS), Q17 (TRAIN/RAIN), Q25 (TAP/PAT), Q105 (SAW/WAS). This is confusing for a 9-year-old because the tip doesn't relate to the question they just answered. Tips are recycled from a pool of ~8, but they should at least be contextually appropriate.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Either (a) assign tips contextually — double-letter tips only appear on questions with repeated letters (Q3/ODD, Q11/CALL, Q109/ROOF, Q110/TELL, Q111/MUDD, Q117/GOOF, Q118/WINN, Q121/ROOTS, Q123/GREET), or (b) expand the tip pool to 15-20 tips and assign randomly from contextually valid subsets.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Near-duplicate questions: both test sports vs art forms
highIssues.description: Q36 (football, rugby, painting, cricket, sculpture) and Q101 (football, cricket, painting, rugby, sculpture) use the same 5 words with only the order changed. Both have the same answer (painting + sculpture) and the same group-of-3 (team sports). This wastes a question slot. A child encountering both will get zero additional practice value.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Replace Q101 with a new D1 question using a different pair of domains — e.g. vegetables vs kitchen utensils, or sea creatures vs land mammals.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Near-duplicate questions: both test days of the week vs months
highIssues.description: Q28 (Monday, March, Friday, August, Wednesday) and Q104 (January, Monday, March, Friday, April) both test the same concept — days of the week vs months. Same grouping logic, same difficulty. Q104 just swaps which are the majority group. This is functionally the same question.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Replace one with a fresh D1 question using different time-related categories — e.g. seasons vs compass directions, or AM/PM times vs calendar events.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: No questions with vowel answers despite Oracle flagging this as a key D3 pattern
highIssues.description: The Oracle specification explicitly states: 'D3 (Hard ~30%): vowel answers, strong distractors that work for one pair but fail on the other'. The lesson content teaches vowel answers (sub-concept 'vowel-shared' with worked examples like TE(?)RM = A → TEA+ARM). However, reviewing all 125 questions, none appear to have a vowel as the correct answer. Every answer is a consonant (T, S, N, R, P, B, D, G, W, M, H, K, L). This is a significant gap because vowel-answer questions are genuinely harder and appear on GL papers.
highIssues.recommendation: Add 5-10 vowel-answer questions at D2-D3 difficulty. Examples from Oracle: SNO(?)ILE / GRA(?)ELL → B (already consonant, wrong example). Better: TE(?)RM / PE(?)ND → A (TEA+ARM, PEA+AND). Also: SE(?)CH / P(?)RT → A (SEA+ACH... no). Need careful construction: FE(?)TH / SP(?)CE → A (FEAT+ATH? no). The lesson already has TE(?)RM example which works.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Duplicate word 'challenge' appears in both setA and setB
highIssues.description: Q97 (absolve + forgive): setA contains ['absolve', 'condemn', 'challenge'] and setB contains ['dispute', 'forgive', 'challenge']. The word 'challenge' appears in both sets. In GL pick-from-sets format, each word should appear in only one set. This is a data integrity issue that could confuse the rendering or the child.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Replace one instance of 'challenge' with a different distractor. In setB, replace 'challenge' with 'forbid' or 'accuse' to maintain the semantic field.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: 'Soggy' and 'damp' presented as synonyms despite intensity difference
highIssues.description: Q76 presents soggy + damp as the correct synonym pair, with explanation stating they 'both mean slightly wet'. However, 'soggy' means thoroughly soaked and unpleasant (much wetter), while 'damp' means slightly moist. These differ significantly in intensity — the same intensity trap the app's own lessons warn about (lesson 'common-synonyms-mistake' teaches that deafening is NOT a synonym of loud because it's too strong). By the app's own teaching, soggy is to damp as deafening is to loud. The explanation incorrectly defines 'soggy' as 'slightly wet' when it means 'very wet and soft'.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Change the correct pair. Either: (a) replace 'soggy' with 'moist' in setA so the pair is moist + damp (true synonyms at the same intensity), or (b) replace 'damp' with 'waterlogged' in setB to pair soggy + waterlogged (both strong-intensity wet).
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q78 and Q103 are identical questions with identical answers
highIssues.description: Both questions ask '___ is to cow as lamb is to ___' with the answer 'calf + sheep'. Q78 (D1) and Q103 (D1) are exact duplicates — same question text, same answer, same difficulty. The only difference is one distractor word (bull vs barn in setA). This wastes a question slot in a 125-question bank.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Delete Q103 and replace with a different young-adult pair at D1. Consider 'piglet + pig', 'duckling + duck', or 'kid + goat'.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q11 and Q122 test identical answer pair 'conductor + team'
highIssues.description: Q11 (D3) asks '___ is to orchestra as captain is to ___' with answer 'conductor + team'. Q122 (D3) asks the identical question '___ is to orchestra as captain is to ___' with the same answer. These are exact duplicates at the same difficulty level. Different distractor words (musician/violin/ship/army vs violin/concert/ship/badge) but the core question and answer are identical.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Delete Q122 and replace with a different leader-to-group pair at D3. Consider 'headteacher + school', 'principal + institution', or 'admiral + fleet'.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q34 and Q112 test identical answer pair 'leaf + flower'
highIssues.description: Q34 (D1) and Q112 (D2) both ask '___ is to tree as petal is to ___' with the answer 'leaf + flower'. The question text is identical. The only difference is the distractor words in the sets and the difficulty rating (D1 vs D2). Having the same question text and answer twice is wasteful.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Delete Q112 and replace with a different part-whole pair at D2. Consider 'pixel + screen', 'carriage + train' (but Q42 already uses this), or 'stanza + poem'.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Several GL-tested relationship types have zero or minimal coverage
highIssues.description: The Oracle lists 22 relationship types for verbal analogies. Of these, the following have zero questions: country associations (e.g. France/French, Japan/yen), container-contents (e.g. wallet/money, vase/flowers), symbol/representation (e.g. dove/peace, crown/royalty), sequence/order (e.g. Monday/Tuesday, spring/summer), grammatical form changes (e.g. run/running, mouse/mice). Collective nouns have only 2 questions (Q9: flock/sheep, Q109: bird/flock). These are all types that appear in GL papers and the gap means children won't practise them.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Add 5-10 questions per missing relationship type, distributed across D1-D3. Priority order: (1) collective nouns (very common in GL), (2) country associations, (3) container-contents, (4) sequence/order. This would require expanding from 125 to ~145-155 questions.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q100 explanation is a confused debugging log — not suitable for children
highIssues.description: Q100 presents 'SHORE → HORSE, PLUM → LUMP. What does CAFE become?' The explanation is a 400-word internal debate analysing different transformation hypotheses, acknowledging failures ('That's not HORSE', 'AFEC isn't a word'), and never arriving at a clear rule. It concludes with 'The simplest common pattern is that they are anagrams'. The answer FACE is correct (CAFE rearranged), but: (1) SHORE→HORSE and PLUM→LUMP follow different rearrangement patterns (not the same positional mapping), and (2) the explanation is completely inappropriate for a child. This is the same issue as Q88 — stream-of-consciousness reasoning left in the explanation field.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Replace explanation with a clear, concise version: 'The pattern rearranges the letters to make a new word (an anagram): SHORE→HORSE, PLUM→LUMP, CAFE→FACE. Each output uses all the same letters in a different order. ✓' However, the question itself is problematic because the rearrangement rule is not systematic (different positional mappings for each pair). Consider replacing with a question that has a clear, consistent transformation rule.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning

highIssues.title: Q97 explanation is a debugging log with incorrect analysis
highIssues.description: Q97 presents 'PEACH → CHEAP, NIGHT → THING. What does ANGEL become?' The explanation is another stream-of-consciousness analysis: 'the last two letters (C,H) move to the front... That gives CHPEA, not CHEAP. Actually, looking more carefully...' It concludes 'Both are anagrams where the letters cycle: positions shift. PEACH→CHEAP takes position 4,5,2,3,1. NIGHT→THING takes position 5,4,2,1,3. These are different mappings, so these are simply anagrams.' The answer GLEAN is correct, but the explanation is unusable for a child.
highIssues.suggestedFix: Replace with: 'The pattern rearranges all the letters: PEACH→CHEAP, NIGHT→THING, ANGEL→GLEAN. Each word is an anagram of the input. ✓' Also review whether anagram questions belong in this topic — the Oracle spec for Word Code Analogies focuses on systematic letter operations, not arbitrary rearrangements. Anagrams might fit better in a dedicated anagram/word puzzle category.
highIssues.subject: verbalreasoning
